news_summary: Breakthroughs in genetic research, gut microbiome therapy, and cancer treatment are paving the way for extended lifespan, rejuvenated liver health, and effective targeting of previously untreatable cancer proteins.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans la recherche génétique, la thérapie axée sur le microbiome intestinal et le traitement du cancer ouvrent la voie à un allongement de la durée de vie, à une amélioration de la santé hépatique et à un ciblage efficace de protéines cancéreuses jusqu'alors incurables.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la investigación genética, la terapia basada en la microbiota intestinal y el tratamiento del cáncer están allanando el camino hacia una mayor esperanza de vida, una mejora de la salud hepática y un tratamiento eficaz de proteínas cancerígenas que antes eran intratables.

articles.title: Scientists successfully transfer longevity gene and extend lifespan
articles.summary: Scientists at the University of Rochester pulled off a remarkable experiment: they transferred a longevity-related gene from the famously long-lived naked mole rat into mice, and the mice ended up healthier and lived longer. The special gene boosts production of a substance called high molecular weight hyaluronic acid, which appears to protect against cancer, reduce inflammation, and support healthier aging. The modified mice showed stronger resistance to tumors, healthier guts, and lower levels of age-related inflammation.
articles.content:
Naked mole rats are not much to look at, but their biology has made them one of the most fascinating animals in aging research. These small, wrinkled rodents can live for decades, rarely develop cancer, and seem unusually protected from many of the diseases that normally arrive with age.

Researchers at the University of Rochester showed that one of those biological advantages can be moved into another mammal. By transferring a gene linked to the naked mole rat's unusually high levels of high molecular weight hyaluronic acid (HMW-HA), the team improved health and modestly extended lifespan in mice.

The work, published in Nature in 2023, suggested that at least some longevity traits that evolved in long-lived animals may be adaptable beyond the species that developed them. The genetically modified mice lived healthier lives and had an approximate 4.4 percent increase in median lifespan compared with ordinary mice.

"Our study provides a proof of principle that unique longevity mechanisms that evolved in long-lived mammalian species can be exported to improve the lifespans of other mammals," says Vera Gorbunova, the Doris Johns Cherry Professor of biology and medicine at Rochester.

Gorbunova, along with Andrei Seluanov, a professor of biology, and their colleagues, focused on a gene that helps produce HMW-HA. This substance is abundant in naked mole rats and has been tied to their striking resistance to cancer, inflammation, and age-related decline.

Why Naked Mole Rats Fascinate Aging Scientists

Naked mole rats are about the size of mice, yet their lifespans are extraordinary for rodents. They can live up to 41 years, nearly ten times longer than similarly sized rodents.

Their long lives are not the only reason scientists study them. As they age, naked mole rats appear to avoid many conditions that commonly affect other mammals, including neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, arthritis, and cancer. For decades, Gorbunova, Seluanov, and other researchers have been investigating how these animals stay so resilient.

One major clue is HMW-HA. Naked mole rats carry roughly ten times more of it than mice and humans. In earlier work, researchers found that when HMW-HA was removed from naked mole rat cells, those cells became more likely to form tumors.

That finding raised a powerful question. If HMW-HA helps naked mole rats resist cancer and age-related damage, could the same mechanism work in a different animal?

Transferring a Naked Mole Rat Longevity Gene

To test the idea, the Rochester team engineered mice to carry the naked mole rat version of the hyaluronan synthase 2 gene. This gene helps make the protein that produces HMW-HA.

All mammals have a version of hyaluronan synthase 2, but the naked mole rat version appears to be especially active. It seems to drive stronger gene expression, leading to greater production of the protective molecule.

The modified mice developed higher levels of hyaluronan in several tissues. They also showed stronger protection against spontaneous tumors and chemically induced skin cancer.

The effects were not limited to cancer resistance. The mice carrying the naked mole rat gene stayed healthier overall, lived longer than regular mice, had less inflammation in multiple tissues as they aged, and maintained better gut health.

Because chronic inflammation is one of the major biological features of aging, the reduction in inflammation was especially important. The researchers believe HMW-HA may work partly by directly influencing the immune system, although more research is needed to explain exactly how it produces such broad benefits.

A Small Lifespan Gain With Big Implications

The increase in median lifespan was about 4.4 percent, which is modest. But the larger significance is that a longevity mechanism from one mammal was successfully transferred to another.

That makes the finding more than a mouse study about a single gene. It supports the idea that nature's long-lived species may contain biological tools that can be studied, adapted, and possibly used to improve health in other animals.

"It took us 10 years from the discovery of HMW-HA in the naked mole rat to showing that HMW-HA improves health in mice," Gorbunova says. "Our next goal is to transfer this benefit to humans."

The researchers believe there may be two main ways to pursue that goal. One would be to slow the breakdown of HMW-HA in the body. Another would be to increase its production.

"We already have identified molecules that slow down hyaluronan degradation and are testing them in pre-clinical trials," Seluanov says. "We hope that our findings will provide the first, but not the last, example of how longevity adaptations from a long-lived species can be adapted to benefit human longevity and health."

Newer Research Adds to the Naked Mole Rat Story

Since the 2023 Nature study, naked mole rats have continued to offer new clues about why they age so differently from other mammals. A 2025 study in Science reported another potential longevity mechanism involving cGAS, a protein better known for its role in immune defense. In humans and mice, cGAS can interfere with some forms of DNA repair, but the naked mole rat version appears to help cells repair DNA damage more effectively. That study found that specific changes in the naked mole rat protein improved genome stability and delayed signs of aging in experimental models.

This newer work does not replace the HMW-HA finding. Instead, it strengthens a broader pattern. Naked mole rats likely owe their unusually long, healthy lives to several overlapping defenses, including cancer resistance, inflammation control, DNA repair, and tissue protection.

For human aging research, that matters. A single molecule is unlikely to become a simple fountain of youth. But each discovery gives scientists another possible route for targeting the biological processes that drive age-related disease.

The 2023 gene transfer study remains a striking proof of concept. A survival strategy that evolved in one of nature's strangest mammals helped mice resist disease, age more smoothly, and live longer. The next challenge is determining whether those same biological tricks can be safely adapted to improve human healthspan.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where a longevity gene from naked mole rats was successfully transferred to mice, resulting in healthier aging and extended lifespan. This discovery has broad implications for aging research and potential human health benefits, with detailed context and evidence provided.
articles.personality_title: Scientists extend mice lifespan by using a gene from naked mole rats
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Naked mole rats live much longer than other rodents and rarely get diseases like cancer. Scientists think their special biology helps them age in a healthier way.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Rochester took a gene from naked mole rats that helps produce a protective substance called high molecular weight hyaluronic acid (HMW-HA). They put this gene into mice. The mice with the new gene lived about 4.4% longer, had fewer tumors, less inflammation, and healthier guts.

**Impact** – This study shows that a gene helping one animal live longer can work in another species. It is the first proof that special traits from long-lived animals might be used to improve health and lifespan in others. The mice’s better health and longer life suggest new ways to fight aging.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to find ways to increase HMW-HA in humans, either by stopping it from breaking down or making more of it. They are already testing medicines that slow down its loss. Future research will check if these changes can help people live healthier lives.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A gene from naked mole rats helped mice live longer and healthier lives, opening new paths to study aging and improve health in other animals, including humans.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques prolongent la vie des souris grâce à un gène de rat-taupe nu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les rats-taupes nus vivent beaucoup plus longtemps que les autres rongeurs et attrapent rarement des maladies comme le cancer. Les scientifiques pensent que leur biologie spéciale les aide à vieillir en meilleure santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’université de Rochester ont transféré un gène des rats-taupes nus qui produit une substance protectrice appelée acide hyaluronique de haut poids moléculaire (HMW-HA) dans des souris. Les souris modifiées ont vécu environ 4,4 % plus longtemps, avec moins de tumeurs, moins d’inflammation et un meilleur état de santé intestinal.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre qu’un gène aidant un animal à vivre plus longtemps peut fonctionner dans une autre espèce. C’est la première preuve que des traits spéciaux d’animaux à longue vie pourraient améliorer la santé et la durée de vie d’autres animaux. La meilleure santé et la vie plus longue des souris ouvrent de nouvelles pistes contre le vieillissement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent trouver des moyens d’augmenter le HMW-HA chez l’humain, soit en ralentissant sa dégradation, soit en en produisant davantage. Ils testent déjà des médicaments qui ralentissent sa perte. Les recherches futures vérifieront si ces changements peuvent aider les gens à vivre en meilleure santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un gène de rat-taupe nu a aidé des souris à vivre plus longtemps et en meilleure santé, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier le vieillissement et améliorer la santé chez d’autres animaux, y compris les humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos logran que ratones vivan más con un gen de topo desnudo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los topos desnudos viven mucho más que otros roedores y rara vez sufren enfermedades como el cáncer. Los científicos creen que su biología especial les ayuda a envejecer de forma más saludable.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Rochester tomaron un gen de los topos desnudos que produce una sustancia protectora llamada ácido hialurónico de alto peso molecular (HMW-HA). Pusieron este gen en ratones. Los ratones modificados vivieron alrededor de un 4,4 % más, con menos tumores, menos inflamación y mejor salud intestinal.

**Impacto** – Este estudio demuestra que un gen que ayuda a un animal a vivir más tiempo puede funcionar en otra especie. Es la primera prueba de que características especiales de animales longevos podrían usarse para mejorar la salud y la vida en otros animales. La mejor salud y vida más larga de los ratones sugieren nuevas formas de combatir el envejecimiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren encontrar formas de aumentar el HMW-HA en humanos, ya sea deteniendo su descomposición o produciendo más. Ya están probando medicinas que ralentizan su pérdida. Las investigaciones futuras verificarán si estos cambios pueden ayudar a las personas a vivir más saludables.

**Frase clave** – Un gen de topo desnudo ayudó a ratones a vivir más y con mejor salud, abriendo nuevos caminos para estudiar el envejecimiento y mejorar la salud en otros animales, incluidos los humanos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, wrinkled naked mole rat and a healthy mouse side by side, connected by a glowing, flowing strand of DNA symbolizing gene transfer, surrounded by soft, natural hues emphasizing vitality and longevity.

articles.title: Scientists reversed liver aging with young gut bacteria in stunning study
articles.summary: Rebooting the gut microbiome with bacteria from youth may help stop aging-related liver damage and even prevent liver cancer, according to new research in mice. Older mice that received their own preserved youthful microbiome showed less inflammation, reduced DNA damage, and no signs of liver cancer. Researchers also found that the treatment suppressed a cancer-linked gene called MDM2, making older mice biologically resemble younger ones.
articles.content:
Scientists may have discovered a powerful new link between the gut microbiome, aging, and liver cancer. New research presented at Digestive Disease Week® (DDW) 2026 suggests that restoring gut bacteria to a more youthful state could help protect the liver, reduce age related damage, and potentially lower cancer risk.

The findings come from a mouse study focused on the microbiome, the vast community of bacteria and other microbes living in the digestive system. Researchers found that giving older mice back their own younger gut microbes produced striking effects throughout the body, especially in the liver.

Young Gut Microbiome Protected Aging Mice

To test the idea, scientists collected fecal samples from eight young mice and preserved them for later use. As the mice aged, the researchers transplanted the stored samples back into the same animals through a process known as fecal microbiota transplantation, or FMT.

Another group of eight aging mice served as controls and received sterilized fecal material instead. Researchers also included a small group of young mice to provide baseline comparisons.

By the end of the study, none of the mice that received their restored youthful microbiome developed liver cancer. In contrast, liver cancer appeared in 2 out of 8 untreated aging mice. The treated mice also showed lower levels of inflammation and reduced liver injury.

"We're learning from this work that the aging microbiome actively contributes to liver dysfunction and cancer risk rather than simply reflecting the aging process," said Qingjie Li, PhD, associate professor in the Division of Gastroenterology and Hepatology at The University of Texas Medical Branch, and lead researcher on the study. "The microbiome has a broader influence on the body's cancer defenses than previously understood."

Researchers Found Changes in a Cancer Related Gene

After completing the in vivo study, the research team closely examined liver tissue from the mice. They discovered important differences involving MDM2, a gene already associated with liver cancer development.

Young mice showed low levels of the MDM2 protein, while untreated older mice had much higher levels. Older mice that received the restored microbiome had suppressed MDM2 levels that more closely resembled those seen in younger animals.

"Restoring a more youthful microbiome can reverse several core features of aging at both the molecular and functional level, including inflammation, fibrosis, mitochondrial decline, telomere attrition, and DNA damage," Dr. Li said.

Earlier Heart Research Led to the Discovery

The liver findings emerged unexpectedly from previous research examining the microbiome's effects on heart health. In that earlier cardiac study, scientists observed that altering gut bacteria appeared to improve heart function.

However, when the researchers later analyzed tissue samples, they noticed even stronger effects in the liver. That observation prompted the team to investigate the connection more deeply.

To reduce the chances of immune complications or infection, the researchers used each mouse's own preserved microbiome rather than relying on donor samples. They said this approach also creates a clearer proof of concept for possible future human studies.

Dr. Li stressed that the findings are limited to animal research and cannot yet be applied to people. Still, he said the team hopes to begin first in human clinical trials in the near future.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where restoring youthful gut bacteria in aging mice reversed liver aging, reduced inflammation, and prevented liver cancer, indicating a potential new approach to combat aging-related diseases with broad health implications. The study is detailed, focused on a single topic, and suggests future human clinical trials, highlighting its meaningful impact on health and aging.
articles.personality_title: Young gut bacteria reverse liver aging and cancer risk in mice study
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been exploring how the gut microbiome, which is the community of bacteria living in our digestive system, affects aging and diseases like liver cancer. Aging often leads to liver damage and increased cancer risk.

**What happened** – Researchers at The University of Texas Medical Branch took gut bacteria samples from young mice and saved them. When these mice grew older, the scientists put their own young bacteria back into their guts using a method called fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT). They compared these treated mice to older mice that did not get the young bacteria.

**Impact** – The older mice that received their youthful bacteria showed less liver inflammation, less DNA damage, and none developed liver cancer. In contrast, some untreated mice did develop liver cancer. The treatment also lowered levels of a gene called MDM2, which is linked to cancer, making the older mice’s livers act more like those of young mice.

**What’s next step** – The researchers want to test this treatment in humans. Since they used each mouse’s own bacteria, it may be safer and easier to try in clinical trials. However, more studies are needed to see if this works for people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Restoring young gut bacteria in older mice reversed signs of liver aging and prevented cancer, pointing to a new way to protect liver health as we age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les bactéries intestinales jeunes inversent le vieillissement du foie et le risque de cancer chez la souris
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient comment le microbiome intestinal, la communauté de bactéries dans notre système digestif, influence le vieillissement et des maladies comme le cancer du foie. Le vieillissement cause souvent des dommages au foie et augmente le risque de cancer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Texas ont prélevé des bactéries intestinales de jeunes souris et les ont conservées. Quand ces souris ont vieilli, les scientifiques leur ont remis leurs propres bactéries jeunes grâce à une méthode appelée transplantation de microbiote fécal (TMF). Ils ont comparé ces souris traitées à des souris âgées non traitées.

**Impact** – Les souris âgées ayant reçu leurs bactéries jeunes ont montré moins d’inflammation du foie, moins de dommages à l’ADN, et aucune n’a développé de cancer du foie. En revanche, certaines souris non traitées ont eu un cancer. Le traitement a aussi réduit un gène appelé MDM2, lié au cancer, rendant le foie des souris âgées plus semblable à celui des jeunes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester ce traitement chez l’humain. Comme ils ont utilisé les propres bactéries des souris, cela pourrait être plus sûr pour les essais cliniques. Mais il faut encore des études pour voir si cela fonctionne chez les personnes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Restaurer les bactéries intestinales jeunes chez des souris âgées a inversé le vieillissement du foie et empêché le cancer, ouvrant une nouvelle voie pour protéger la santé du foie avec l’âge.
articles.personality_title_es: Bacterias jóvenes del intestino revierten envejecimiento y riesgo de cáncer de hígado en ratones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian cómo el microbioma intestinal, la comunidad de bacterias en nuestro sistema digestivo, afecta el envejecimiento y enfermedades como el cáncer de hígado. El envejecimiento suele causar daño hepático y mayor riesgo de cáncer.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Texas tomaron muestras de bacterias intestinales de ratones jóvenes y las guardaron. Cuando estos ratones envejecieron, les devolvieron sus propias bacterias jóvenes mediante un método llamado trasplante de microbiota fecal (TMF). Compararon estos ratones tratados con ratones mayores que no recibieron el tratamiento.

**Impacto** – Los ratones mayores que recibieron sus bacterias jóvenes tuvieron menos inflamación en el hígado, menos daño en el ADN y ninguno desarrolló cáncer de hígado. En cambio, algunos ratones no tratados sí desarrollaron cáncer. El tratamiento también redujo un gen llamado MDM2, relacionado con el cáncer, haciendo que el hígado de los ratones mayores se comportara más como el de los jóvenes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar este tratamiento en humanos. Como usaron las propias bacterias de cada ratón, puede ser más seguro para ensayos clínicos. Pero se necesitan más estudios para saber si funciona en personas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Restaurar bacterias intestinales jóvenes en ratones mayores revirtió signos de envejecimiento hepático y previno el cáncer, señalando una nueva forma de proteger la salud del hígado con la edad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a healthy, vibrant mouse silhouette surrounded by glowing, youthful gut bacteria depicted as softly illuminated, interconnected microscopic shapes flowing gently toward a stylized, regenerating liver symbolized by smooth, bright, organic forms with subtle hints of restored cellular patterns and DNA strands, all rendered in natural, soft earth tones and muted greens.

articles.title: ‘Undruggable’ cancer proteins meet their match
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01447-2People with a deadly form of pancreatic cancer survive longer on a drug that blocks the activity of a family of mutant proteins.
articles.content: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a drug effectively targets previously 'undruggable' mutant proteins in pancreatic cancer, leading to longer survival for patients. This represents a meaningful advancement in cancer treatment with broad implications for public health.
articles.personality_title: New drug extends survival for pancreatic cancer patients by targeting hard-to-treat proteins
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Pancreatic cancer is one of the deadliest types of cancer. Many patients have mutations in certain proteins that make the cancer grow quickly. These mutant proteins have been called “undruggable” because no medicines could block them effectively.

**What happened** – Scientists have developed a new drug that blocks the activity of these mutant proteins in pancreatic cancer. In recent studies, patients who took this drug lived longer than those who did not. The drug works by specifically targeting the family of mutant proteins that were previously impossible to treat.

**Impact** – This is important because it offers a new way to fight a cancer that is usually very hard to treat and often leads to early death. By blocking these mutant proteins, the drug slows down cancer growth and improves patient survival. This breakthrough could change how doctors treat pancreatic cancer.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue testing the drug in more patients to confirm its benefits and check for any side effects. They will also explore if the drug can help with other cancers that have similar mutant proteins.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new drug that blocks previously untreatable proteins helps people with pancreatic cancer live longer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau médicament prolonge la survie des patients atteints de cancer du pancréas en ciblant des protéines difficiles à traiter
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer du pancréas est l’un des cancers les plus mortels. Beaucoup de patients ont des mutations dans certaines protéines qui font croître rapidement le cancer. Ces protéines mutantes étaient qualifiées « d’indruggables » car aucun médicament ne pouvait les bloquer efficacement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé un nouveau médicament qui bloque l’activité de ces protéines mutantes dans le cancer du pancréas. Dans des études récentes, les patients ayant pris ce médicament ont vécu plus longtemps que ceux qui ne l’ont pas pris. Ce médicament cible spécifiquement la famille de protéines mutantes auparavant impossibles à traiter.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela offre une nouvelle façon de combattre un cancer souvent très difficile à traiter et qui cause souvent une mort rapide. En bloquant ces protéines mutantes, le médicament ralentit la croissance du cancer et améliore la survie des patients. Cette avancée pourrait changer la façon dont les médecins traitent le cancer du pancréas.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à tester ce médicament sur plus de patients pour confirmer ses bénéfices et vérifier d’éventuels effets secondaires. Ils étudieront aussi si ce médicament peut aider pour d’autres cancers avec des protéines mutantes similaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau médicament qui bloque des protéines jusque-là intraitables aide les personnes atteintes de cancer du pancréas à vivre plus longtemps.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo medicamento prolonga la vida de pacientes con cáncer de páncreas al atacar proteínas difíciles de tratar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer de páncreas es uno de los tipos de cáncer más mortales. Muchos pacientes tienen mutaciones en ciertas proteínas que hacen que el cáncer crezca rápidamente. Estas proteínas mutantes se llamaban “no tratables” porque ningún medicamento podía bloquearlas bien.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron un nuevo medicamento que bloquea la actividad de estas proteínas mutantes en el cáncer de páncreas. En estudios recientes, los pacientes que tomaron este medicamento vivieron más tiempo que quienes no lo tomaron. El medicamento actúa específicamente sobre la familia de proteínas mutantes que antes no se podían tratar.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ofrece una nueva manera de combatir un cáncer que suele ser muy difícil de tratar y que a menudo causa la muerte rápida. Al bloquear estas proteínas mutantes, el medicamento frena el crecimiento del cáncer y mejora la supervivencia de los pacientes. Este avance podría cambiar cómo los médicos tratan el cáncer de páncreas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán probando el medicamento en más pacientes para confirmar sus beneficios y revisar posibles efectos secundarios. También explorarán si el medicamento puede ayudar con otros tipos de cáncer que tengan proteínas mutantes similares.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo medicamento que bloquea proteínas antes intratables ayuda a personas con cáncer de páncreas a vivir más tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of intricate, glowing puzzle pieces fitting perfectly together amid a softly illuminated network of tangled, dark strands symbolizing previously "undruggable" cancer proteins being unlocked and neutralized, rendered in gentle earth tones and muted blues.
